stem: A pupil writes the Boolean expression `Q = A AND (B OR NOT C)` and is asked to read it in plain English.
model_answer: Q is true when A is true AND at least one of two things is true: B, or C is false (because NOT C reverses C). For example, with A=1, B=0, C=0: NOT C = 1, B OR NOT C = 1, A AND 1 = 1, so Q = 1. With A=0, the AND clause forces Q = 0 regardless of the rest.
review_notes: Teacher-marked. Reward correct precedence (NOT before AND/OR, brackets first) and a worked example with values that illustrates the meaning.

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Explain in words what `Q = A AND (B OR NOT C)` evaluates to. Include a worked example with values for A, B and C.

mark_points.text: explains that Q is 1 only when A is 1 AND (B is 1 OR C is 0)
gives a worked example using specific values that produces a defensible Q

misconceptions.label: Ignoring NOT precedence
misconceptions.description: NOT applies to a single operand. `B OR NOT C` is `B OR (NOT C)`, not `NOT (B OR C)`.
